en: Good pronunciation is about three main things.
Your sounds, the stresses in your words and your sentences, and your linking.
How you connect the words.
And my next few videos are all going to be about how you link your sounds.
To speak fluently, your words need to flow.
So they need to be connected.
If your words are not connected, then you will sound strange.
It may be easy for people to understand you, but you will not be speaking in a natural way.
So you need to connect your sounds.
So make sure you're following me, or you'll miss them.
If you want to speak fluently, you need to connect your words.
So here's a good tip for connecting those words.
If the last sound of this word is the same as the first sound of this word,
you only pronounce that sound once.
For example, autumn morning.
Look at this beautiful autumn morning.
It's not autumn morning, but it's connected.
Autumn morning. You might say, I spent ten pounds.
I spent ten pounds, so I need some money.
Some money. She's a big girl.
She's a big girl. I want to go.
I want to go. I spent nine nights.
Nine nights.
So if you have the last sound of this word is the same sound as the beginning of this one,
you only pronounce it once. Give it a try and follow me.

Here's another linking tip so you can link your words when you speak
so you sound more fluent by constantly speaking
and sounding more like a native speaker with no gaps in your words.
If this word ends with a consonant sound and this word begins with a vowel sound,
we pronounce that sound here. We pronounce that here.

Linking. This is an important part of your pronunciation
because it's how one word connects to the next word when you're speaking.
Now, if this word ends in an O or an OO sound
and the next word begins with a vowel sound,
we connect them by inserting a W sound.
This is because your lips are going O or OO.
O and OO. So you follow through with that into the next word.
You already do this with words like going and doing.
So going and doing. We have this W in the middle
and we use the same idea to connect lots of other words.

So if this word finishes with an A, E or I sound
and the next word begins with a vowel sound,
we insert a Y to connect those words.
For example, we don't say grey eyes,
but grey eyes, grey eyes.
Not my arm, but my arm, my arm.

You can say it all together.

Or we can say three evenings, three evenings.

If a word finishes with an A or an R sound
and the next word begins with a vowel sound,
we link those words with a R.
For example, instead of car insurance,
car insurance, car insurance.
